did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a novel convolutional neural network (CNN) framework based on atrous convolutions for automatic lesion segmentation in dermoscopic skin cancer images. The deep learning model was trained and evaluated on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets to segment skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH Open Access' and presents original experimental work. It proposes a novel convolutional neural network (DilatedSkinNet) for automatic lesion segmentation, details the methodology (architecture, hyperparameters, datasets like ISIC 2016-2018), and reports original quantitative results (Jaccard index, Accuracy, Dice score) comparing the proposed model against state-of-the-art methods and challenge winners.

title: Automatic lesion segmentation using atrous convolutional deep neural networks in dermoscopic skin cancer images
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automatic skin lesion segmentation in dermoscopic images to aid in the early detection of melanoma. The authors propose DilatedSkinNet, a novel convolutional neural network framework based on atrous convolutions, which incorporates batch normalization, leakyReLU activations, and fine-tuned hyperparameters. The model was trained and evaluated on three benchmark datasets from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC): ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, and ISIC 2018. Evaluation metrics included the Jaccard index, with the network achieving average scores of 90.4%, 81.8%, and 89.1% on the respective datasets. The results demonstrated that the proposed method outperformed the top three winners of the ISIC challenges and other state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, the model successfully extracts lesions in a single pass without requiring pre-processing steps.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the automatic segmentation of skin lesions in dermoscopic images. The datasets used (ISIC 2016, 2017, 2018) contain various types of skin lesions, but the paper treats the lesion boundary as a binary segmentation target (lesion vs. background) rather than classifying specific histological types.
Mentioned as the most dangerous and aggressive form of skin cancer and the primary motivation for the research, but not explicitly segmented or classified as a distinct class in the experiments.
Listed in the introduction as one of the different types of skin cancer, but not studied or segmented in the experiments.
Listed in the introduction as one of the different types of skin cancer, but not studied or segmented in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Lesion boundary mask (binary segmentation of lesion vs. background)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes 'DilatedSkinNet' for 'automatic lesion segmentation' in dermoscopic images. The model performs pixel classification into two classes: lesion and background. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics: Jaccard index (JAC), Dice score (DICE), and Accuracy (ACC). The task is explicitly defined as semantic segmentation of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of skin cancer types including Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous, and Nevus, with ground truth annotations.

use.training: Used for training the DilatedSkinNet model (split from original 900 images at 7:3 ratio).
use.validation: Used for validation (split from original training set at 7:3 ratio).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of skin cancer types including Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous, and Nevus, with ground truth annotations.

use.training: Used for training the DilatedSkinNet model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DilatedSkinNet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DilatedSkinNet model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of skin cancer types including Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous, and Nevus, with ground truth annotations.

use.training: Used for training the DilatedSkinNet model (original 2594 training images split, with 30% used for testing).
use.validation: Used for validation of the DilatedSkinNet model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DilatedSkinNet model (derived from 30% of original training data as external test data was not provided).

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset widely used in literature studies, included to evaluate the robustness of the model trained on ISIC 2018.

use.testing: Used for external evaluation/robustness testing using the network trained on ISIC 2018.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (Accuracy, Jaccard index, Dice coefficient) and qualitative statements about performance margins (e.g., 'higher performance with a margin of (±1%)'). No statistical significance testing was employed.

ISIC 2016.notes: DilatedSkinNet achieved the highest JAC index among challenge winners and recent studies. Training DilatedSkinNet on ISIC 2018 and testing on ISIC 2016 showed higher performance (ACC increased from 94.0 to 95.0%) compared to training on ISIC 2016 alone.

ISIC 2017.notes: DilatedSkinNet outperformed state-of-the-art methods and challenge winners. Training on ISIC 2018 and testing on ISIC 2017 showed higher performance (ACC increased from 87.9 to 88.8%) compared to training on ISIC 2017 alone.

ISIC 2018.notes: DilatedSkinNet achieved the highest JAC index among challenge winners and recent studies. The model generalized well to other datasets when trained on ISIC 2018.

PH 2.notes: Used for external evaluation/robustness testing. The network trained on ISIC 2018 was evaluated on this dataset.

architecture_details.backbone: Custom architecture built from scratch using atrous/dilated convolutions
architecture_details.layers: 16 convolutional layers organized into five building blocks
components: Convolutional layers with varying dilation factors
Batch Normalization layers placed after each convolutional layer
1x1 convolutional layer for flattening feature maps and minimizing channels
architecture_details.output_head: SoftMax layer producing probability scores for two classes (lesion and background)
architecture_details.training_schedule: Trained on ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2018 datasets separately; also trained on ISIC 2018 and tested on others for generalization
architecture_details.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported (compared to SegNet 29M, UNet 31M, DeepLabV3+ 20M)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images were downsized to 192 × 256 × 3 dimensions using nearest-neighbor interpolation technique to fit network input requirements while maintaining aspect ratio and resolution.
Data Augmentation: Applied rotation at angles between 15° and 20°, scaling, and translation with factors [-6, 5] to enlarge the training dataset and prevent overfitting/underfitting.
No Pre-processing: The model extracts lesions from the whole image in one pass requiring no pre-processing step (e.g., no hair removal, color correction, or denoising was applied before modeling).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to enlarge the training dataset.

conclusions: The proposed DilatedSkinNet network achieved high segmentation accuracy, with average Jaccard indices of 90.4% on ISIC 2016, 81.8% on ISIC 2017, and 89.1% on ISIC 2018.

The proposed method outperformed the top three winners of the ISIC challenges and other state-of-the-art semantic segmentation methods (such as U-Net, SegNet, and DeepLabv3+) in terms of performance metrics.
The model successfully extracts lesions from the whole image in a single pass with low inference time (9-14 seconds) without requiring any pre-processing steps.

The network demonstrated good generalization capabilities, showing improved performance (margin of ±1%) when trained on the ISIC 2018 dataset and tested on ISIC 2016 and 2017 test sets compared to training on individual datasets.
The network maintained robustness when evaluated on the external PH 2 dataset, achieving a Jaccard index of 89.1%.

The use of atrous convolutions, leakyReLU activation functions, and data augmentation contributed to higher performance and faster learning compared to modified architectures lacking these components.
important_remarks: The ISIC 2018 dataset lacked external test data, so the authors used 30% of the training set as the test set, which introduces potential data leakage or optimistic bias compared to standard external validation protocols.
The ISIC 2016 dataset also lacked external validation data, requiring the authors to split the training set (7:3 ratio) for validation, which reduces the effective training data size.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely on descriptive statistics and point estimates.
The model was implemented in MATLAB, which is less common for deep learning research compared to frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow, potentially affecting reproducibility and community adoption.

The study focuses on binary segmentation (lesion vs. background) and does not classify specific histological types (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus), limiting direct clinical diagnostic utility without additional classification steps.
The PH2 dataset was used for external robustness testing, but the number of images and specific split details were not reported, making the extent of external validation difficult to assess.
The paper reports inference times (9-14 seconds) which are relatively high for real-time clinical deployment, although faster than some comparators like SegNet (33s).

The model achieved high performance but the abstract and results emphasize that it outperformed challenge winners; however, the margin of improvement over the closest competitor (DeepLabv3+) was small (e.g., JAC 0.904 vs 0.892 on ISIC 2016).
